question: How do students join the Office Hours or live workshop sessions if the Zoom link isn’t public?
answer_llm:
Students don’t use the Zoom link directly. The Zoom link is only for instructors/presenters/TAs.

For students:
- Join via the YouTube Live stream
- Submit questions in Slido
- Find the video link in the announcements channel on Telegram or Slack before the session starts
- You can also watch on the DataTalksClub YouTube channel

Don’t post questions in chat, since they may be missed.

The zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs.

You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: How do I find my own entry on the leaderboard if I can’t spot my name?
answer_llm: Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link. Your account was automatically given a random display name, so that link will take you straight to your own entry.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why does the leaderboard show some random name for me instead of my real one?
answer_llm:
Because the leaderboard uses your **public display name**, not your certificate name.

When you create your account, you’re assigned a **random anonymous nickname** by default, like “Lucid Elbakyan.” That’s what shows on the public leaderboard. Your **real name** is only used in the **certificate name** field and appears on your certificate, not publicly.

If you want to change it, go to **Edit Course Profile**:
- **First field** = leaderboard/display name
- **Second field** = certificate/official name

question: Can I take the course on my own time and still get a certificate at the end?
answer_llm: No. You can take the course in self-paced mode, but you won’t get a certificate that way. Certificates are only awarded if you finish with a live cohort, because you need to peer-review other projects during the course.
answer_orig:
No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

We don't award certificates for the self-paced mode. The reason is you need to peer-review 3 capstone(s) after submitting your project.

You can only peer-review projects at the time the course is running; after the form is closed and the peer-review list is compiled.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing from one day to the next?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means it is not ready yet.

The homework is only final when:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change, so working on it early is at your own risk.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why don't we use LangChain in this course instead of writing everything ourselves?
answer_llm:
LangChain is a framework for building LLM apps, but in this course we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

The idea is to learn the fundamentals before using a framework that automates them — like learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before React or Angular.

LangChain is a framework for building LLM-powered apps. In this course, we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API error about insufficient credits when using `responses.create`?
answer_llm:
You get that error because your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough quota/credits. The `responses.create` call is failing with an “insufficient credits” / “insufficient_quota” error.

To fix it:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account billing, then try again.
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
3. You may need to create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you want, I can also help you check whether this is an OpenAI billing issue or an OpenRouter quota issue.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does the `OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits` message mean in `responses.create`?
answer_llm:
It means your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough quota/credits to run `responses.create`.

In other words, the request is being rejected because your account has insufficient billing credits. To fix it, add credits to your OpenAI account, then try again.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: Is there a reason my OpenAI `responses.create` call fails even though the command looks fine?
answer_llm:
Yes — a common reason is **insufficient credits or quota**, even if the `responses.create` command itself looks correct.

If you’re using **OpenRouter**, there’s another frequent issue: calling `responses.create()` without a reasonable `max_output_tokens` can trigger a **402 error** because OpenRouter checks against the maximum possible output size.

A typical fix is to set:

```python
max_output_tokens=1024
```

If you’re using OpenRouter, also make sure your `.env` is configured correctly, for example:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If it still fails, check your key’s remaining credits and make sure you’re using a pinned model rather than `openrouter/free`.

If the issue is with direct OpenAI, the error is usually due to **insufficient credits**.

question: How can I fix the error I see when running `responses.create` in OpenAI about credits?
answer_llm:
If you’re seeing an OpenAI `responses.create` error about credits, it usually means your API account has **insufficient quota/credits**.

Fix it by:

1. **Adding billing credits** to your OpenAI account  
   - OpenAI API has no free usage.
   - Add funds in your billing settings, then try again.

2. **Make sure you’re using a model available to your account**  
   - For example, in `responses.create(model='gpt-4o', ...)`, choose a model you have access to.

3. **If needed, recreate your API key** after adding credits and update it locally.

If you’re actually using **OpenRouter** through `responses.create`, the fix is often to **set a smaller `max_output_tokens`**, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you want, I can help you tell whether your error is from **OpenAI billing** or **OpenRouter 402 limits**.

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI, and how do I fix it?
answer_llm:
You’re getting a `429 RateLimitError` because your OpenAI account has hit its **quota / billing limit** — in this case it’s usually **`insufficient_quota`**, not just “too many requests.”

How to fix it:
1. **Add billing/credits** to your OpenAI account (pay-as-you-go), minimum about **$5**.
2. Make sure you’re using a **model available to your account** in `responses.create(...)`.
3. You may need to **create a new API key** after adding credits and update it locally.
4. Re-run your code.

If you’re seeing 429s during the course from lots of requests, also reduce API usage:
- lower concurrency,
- use retries with backoff,
- cache results,
- batch inputs,
- or use a cheaper model like `gpt-4o-mini`.

If the error specifically says **`insufficient_quota`**, it’s a billing issue, not something retries alone will solve.

question: Why does the course recommend uv over pip and virtualenv for setup?
answer_llm:
The course recommends `uv` because it’s **fast and convenient**. It’s an **all-in-one tool** that replaces `pip`, `virtualenv`, `pip-tools`, `pipx`, `poetry`, `pyenv`, and `twine`, so instead of using several separate tools, you can manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies with one fast command and reproducible lockfiles.

You **don’t have to use it** — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work — but `uv` makes setup **simpler and faster**, which is why the lessons use it.

question: How do I keep my API keys in a .env file and read them in Python?
answer_llm:
Store your keys in a `.env` file, add that file to `.gitignore`, and load it in Python with `python-dotenv`.

### 1) Create a `.env` file
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

### 2) Ignore it in Git
```gitignore
.env
```

### 3) Install `python-dotenv`
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

### 4) Read the keys in Python
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

If you want, I can also show you how to do this in Jupyter Notebook or with a `.env.example` file.

question: How can I plug Google Gemini into the Module 1 notebook if I want to use the OpenAI-style API instead of the usual setup?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI-compatible Gemini endpoint in the Module 1 notebook like this:

1. Put your Gemini API key in `.env` as `GEMINI_API_KEY`
2. Create an `OpenAI` client with Google’s base URL
3. Call a Gemini model by name in `chat.completions.create`

Example:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

A couple of important notes:
- This uses the older `chat.completions` style, not the newer Responses API.
- You may need to adjust the notebook code where it reads the model output and handles tools/function calls.

If you want, I can also show how to adapt the notebook’s tool-calling code for Gemini.

question: How do I install Ollama on Mac, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
Install Ollama from the official download page: https://ollama.com/download

- **macOS**: download the `.pkg` file and install it.
- **Windows**: download the `.msi` file and install it.
- **Linux**: run:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```
answer_orig:
First, install Ollama by visiting [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and choosing your operating system:

- **macOS**: Download the `.pkg` and install it.
- **Windows**: Download the `.msi` and install it.
- **Linux**: Run the following command in the terminal:

  ```bash
  curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This command will:

- Download the LLaMA 3 model (~4GB).
- Start the model locally.
- Open a chat-like interface where you can type questions.

To test the Ollama local server, run the following command:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

You should receive a response similar to:

```json
{"models": [...]}  
```

Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

Here is a minimal Python example:

```python
import ollama

response = ollama.chat(
    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

question: Why does tiktoken show fewer tokens than the OpenAI API for the same prompt?
answer_llm:
`tiktoken` only counts the tokens in the text itself, while the OpenAI API also adds internal chat-formatting tokens.

In particular:

- each chat message adds about **4 structural tokens** for role/content/separators
- the API adds **2 tokens** to mark the start of assistant generation
- extra whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can also change the count slightly

So it’s normal for `tiktoken` to show fewer tokens than the API for the same prompt.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: What extra tokens does chat.completions.create add behind the scenes?
answer_llm:
`chat.completions.create()` adds:

- **4 structural tokens per message**  
  (for role, content, separators, etc.)
- **2 extra global tokens**  
  to mark the start of assistant response generation

So the overhead is typically **4 × number of messages + 2 tokens**.

question: How can I set my API key once so I don’t have to export it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It loads your `.env` automatically when you enter the project directory.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# in your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project folder.

If you already use `direnv`, that’s also fine.

question: How do I decide on field weights in minsearch or a similar search engine?
answer_llm:
The systematic way is to evaluate different field weights against a ground-truth dataset.

A practical approach:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weights.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR.

For small projects, you can also tune weights by trial and error, but evaluation is the more reliable method.

question: What’s the best way to clean documents before using them for RAG, and what kinds of junk should I remove?
answer_llm:
The best way is to make the documents **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

Remove junk like:
- broken HTML
- duplicate text
- boilerplate
- OCR errors
- repeated headers
- repeated footers

At the same time, **keep useful context** such as:
- titles
- section names
- dates
- page numbers
- speaker names
- Q&A structure

Then store the cleaned result in a structured format, chunk it so related context stays together, and keep metadata for later filtering or ranking.
answer_orig:
Prepare the data so it is clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve.

Common steps:

- Remove obvious noise such as broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers, and repeated footers.
- Preserve useful context such as titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the result in a structured format that is easy to process. JSON is often convenient, but it is not mandatory.
- Chunk the documents in a way that keeps related context together.
- Keep metadata that may help filtering or ranking later.

The exact format depends on the source data. The goal is not just to make the text shorter, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, why does uv show “Failed to hardlink files” — is that actually a problem?
answer_llm:
No. In GitHub Codespaces, that warning usually means `uv` can’t hardlink files from its cache into the environment, so it falls back to copying them instead.

The package still installs correctly, so it’s not a real problem.

If you want to suppress the warning, you can set:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: If uv can’t hardlink from cache in Codespaces, will the install still work or does it break?
answer_llm:
No, it won’t break. If `uv` can’t hardlink from the cache in Codespaces, it still installs the package and falls back to copying the files.

You may see a warning, but it’s not an error.

question: If I save code in GitHub Codespaces but never commit it, where does it actually live?

The code is saved inside the Codespaces Linux VM.

question: Does my work in Codespaces disappear if I just close it without committing?

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory than what I have, even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
WSL2 is only using the amount of RAM allocated to it, not all the RAM in your PC. So even if your computer has enough memory, WSL2 may still report that only **Y.Y GiB** is available inside WSL.

To fix it, increase WSL2’s memory limit by creating a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

with something like:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, check the available memory in WSL with `free`.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: My GitHub Codespace is stuck on "Finishing up" and never opens — what should I try first?
answer_llm:
Try this first:

1. Go to **github.com/codespaces**
2. **Stop** the codespace
3. **Start** it again

If it still gets stuck on **“Finishing up”**, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try another browser like **Chrome, Edge, or Brave**.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: If my Codespace won’t reconnect, is there a way to fix it without deleting everything?
answer_llm:
Yes. You can usually try a few fixes before deleting the codespace:

1. Go to **github.com/codespaces**
2. **Stop** the codespace
3. **Start** it again
4. If it still won’t reconnect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try another browser like **Edge, Chrome, or Brave**

Only if those don’t work, delete it and create a new one.

question: Why did my setup disappear after restarting or rebuilding the Codespace?
answer_llm:
Because a **restart/stop-start** should normally keep your `/workspaces` repo, but a **rebuild** or a **brand-new Codespace** starts from a clean image. That means any **system/global installs outside your project** may disappear.

- use `uv sync` to reinstall dependencies
- use `uv add ...` for any extra packages

Also, make sure to **commit and push your work often**, since **uncommitted changes are lost** if you delete or recreate the Codespace.

question: Do files in `/workspaces` survive a stop/start, and what gets lost when I recreate the codespace?

- **System/global installs** outside your project
- Any changes that were **not committed** to git

So the safe rule is: **commit and push often**. Uncommitted work survives stop/start, but it is lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

question: Can I skip OpenAI and use another LLM API for this part of the course?
answer_llm:
Yes. You can use another LLM provider/API instead of OpenAI, and the course is not tied to OpenAI.

For this part of the course, you can use:
- OpenAI-compatible hosted providers like Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, or Mistral
- Open-source models via Hugging Face
- Local models with Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio, etc.

If the provider has an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, you usually only need to change the `base_url`, API key, and model name.

So: **yes, you can skip OpenAI and use another LLM API.**
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: What do I need to change in the course code if I want to use a different provider like Groq or Mistral?
answer_llm:
You usually only need to change:

- `base_url`
- the API key
- the model name

The course code already uses the OpenAI client, so for an OpenAI-compatible provider like Groq or Mistral, that’s typically enough. If the provider uses a different response format or token usage fields, you may also need to adapt those parts of the code.

question: In agentic RAG, what do people mean by a tool or function, and what is it used for?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a **tool/function** is something the model can **call to get external help**.

It’s used for tasks like:
- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The key idea is that in **agentic RAG**, the model can **decide when to use the tool**. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.
answer_orig:
In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: How is tool use in agentic RAG different from the retrieval step in basic RAG?
answer_llm:
In **agentic RAG**, the model can **decide when to call a tool/function** to get external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, or calling an API.

In **basic RAG**, retrieval is usually a **fixed step done before the model answers**.

So the main difference is:
- **Agentic RAG:** retrieval/tool use is **dynamic and model-driven**
- **Basic RAG:** retrieval is a **predefined pipeline step**

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I try `client.responses.create(...)` with a non-OpenAI model?
answer_llm:
Because `client.responses.create(...)` uses OpenAI’s **Responses API**, and that API is **OpenAI-specific**. Non-OpenAI providers like Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc. don’t implement it, so the object you get back isn’t the expected Responses object and `response.output` fails with:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

For non-OpenAI models, use the **chat-completions API** instead:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # adapt to provider requirements
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

If you need structured output with a chat-completions-compatible provider, use `chat.completions.parse` rather than `responses.parse`.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: I'm using Azure OpenAI and keep getting Error code 400 saying missing required parameter tools[0].function. What am I supposed to change in my tool JSON?
For the weather tool in Module 1 Agentic RAG, what should the JSON structure look like so Azure OpenAI accepts it?
My function calling setup fails with tools[0].function missing. How do I define the get_weather tool correctly?
In the FAQ example about Azure OpenAI, what exact update is needed for get_weather_tool to fix the 400 invalid_request_error?
If Azure OpenAI says the function parameter is missing for the first tool, what is the proper get_weather_tool definition I should use?
Why do I get a 404 with toyaikit when I switch from OpenAI to Groq, and what should I use instead?
answer_llm:
Because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` uses OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **OpenAI-only**. When you switch to Groq, you get a 404 because Groq doesn’t implement that endpoint.

Use the **chat completions** classes instead:

- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

For Anthropic, use:

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

If you want, I can also show the exact Groq setup snippet.
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: How can I set up toyaikit with Groq using an OpenAI-compatible base URL and the right runner/client classes?

```python
import os

)

    client=groq_client,
)
```

question: What’s the difference between the Responses endpoint and chat completions in toyaikit, and why does it matter for non-OpenAI providers?
answer_llm:
The key difference is the API shape:

- **Responses endpoint**: `client.responses.create(...)`
  - Used by toyaikit’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient`
  - Reads output from `response.output`
  - This is **OpenAI-specific**

- **Chat completions**: `client.chat.completions.create(...)`
  - Used by toyaikit’s `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner` / `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
  - Reads output from `response.choices[0].message.content`
  - This is the more **widely supported, OpenAI-compatible** interface

Why it matters for non-OpenAI providers:

- Providers like **Groq, Anthropic via compatible wrappers, Mistral, Gemini**, etc. may **not implement OpenAI’s Responses API**
- If toyaikit uses the Responses endpoint with them, you can get errors like **404** or attribute errors
- Switching to **chat completions** lets you use providers that support the standard OpenAI-compatible chat API

So in practice:

- **Use Responses** if you are staying with OpenAI’s own API
- **Use chat completions** if you want to work with non-OpenAI providers through an OpenAI-compatible endpoint

This matters because it determines whether your agent code will actually run on providers like Groq or Anthropic without modification.

question: Why am I getting APIStatusError 402 from OpenRouter when I call responses.create, and how do I stop it?
answer_llm:
OpenRouter can return **APIStatusError 402** on `responses.create()` when you **don’t set a reasonable `max_output_tokens`**.

Why it happens:
- OpenRouter checks billing/limits against the **maximum possible output**.
- If that max is large by default, a free or low-limit key may be rejected **before the model runs**.
- This is different from OpenAI’s usual **429** for quota issues.

How to stop it:
Set a lower `max_output_tokens`, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.

Also make sure your `.env` points to OpenRouter, for example:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If it still fails:
- Check your OpenRouter key limits/credits
- Prefer a pinned model like `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free` instead of `openrouter/free`
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

question: In the Module 1 homework, does RAG need to search the split chunks instead of the full docs?
answer_llm:
Yes. In Module 1 homework, RAG should search the **split chunks**, not the full documents.

So you should fit the index on `chunks`:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not on `documents`:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

Then the pipeline retrieves relevant chunks and includes only those chunks in the prompt.
answer_orig:
It means you should build and search the index using the chunked documents, not the original full documents.

For example, if your original records are in `documents` and your split records are in `chunks`, fit the search index on `chunks`:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

Then the RAG pipeline retrieves relevant chunks and puts only those chunks into the prompt. This is what reduces the amount of context sent to the model.

question: For homework, do I need my numeric result to match one of the choices exactly?
answer_llm: No. If your numeric answer is close to one of the choices, you should choose the closest option.

- Different dataset versions.

question: How should I get token counts for the Module 1 homework if I'm using a different LLM provider?
answer_llm:
Get the token count from the model’s **response object**.

If you’re using a non-OpenAI provider, check its returned **usage fields** and adapt your code accordingly. Don’t use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, etc., because tokenization differs by model.

If the provider doesn’t expose token usage, use that provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.
answer_orig:
For the current Module 1 homework, get the token count from the model response object.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why is my homework link getting rejected with a 500 or other non-200 error, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
The checker is trying to **GET your submitted URL** and expects an **HTTP 200** response. If it gets a **500** or any non-200, the link is usually not publicly reachable as-is.

What to check first:

- **Make sure the repo is public**
- **Remove a trailing `.git`** if you included one
- **Double-check for a typo in the URL**

A quick test: open the link in a **private/incognito window**. If it loads there, it should work for the checker too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

answer_llm:

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we turn text into numbers?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are a way to turn text into numbers while preserving meaning.

Why we do it:
- Computers can’t directly compare text by meaning.
- By converting text into vectors of numbers, we can measure how similar two pieces of text are using math.
- Similar texts should end up with similar vectors, so their positions in vector space are close together.

In vector search, this lets us:
- compare a query to many documents efficiently,
- find semantically related content,
- use operations like dot product or cosine similarity to rank results.

So, we turn text into numbers because numbers let us do fast mathematical similarity search, while still keeping the semantic meaning of the original text.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings keep the meaning of similar inputs close to each other?
answer_llm: Embeddings keep similar inputs close by converting non-numerical data into numerical vectors while preserving meaning and context. So if two inputs are similar, the embedding algorithm should produce similar numerical data, and their proximity in vector space reflects that similarity.

answer_llm:

An **embedding** is the **numeric vector representation** of some text or other data.  
A **vector space model** is the **framework/idea** of representing items as vectors so you can compare them mathematically, often by cosine similarity or dot product.

So in practice:

- **Embeddings** = the vectors
- **Vector space model** = the approach that uses those vectors

The context says related concepts include the “vector space model,” so they’re definitely connected, but an embedding is not quite the same thing as the whole vector space model.

question: What does dimensionality reduction have to do with embeddings and similarity?
answer_llm:
Dimensionality reduction is related because embeddings turn data into vectors, and dimensionality reduction can compress those vectors into fewer dimensions while trying to preserve the important structure or meaning.

- similarity measures like cosine similarity compare how close or aligned those vectors are
- dimensionality reduction can make those vectors smaller and faster to work with, while still keeping similar items close enough for similarity search

question: Why did the lesson use a plain dot product for cosine similarity, but the homework says to normalize first?
answer_llm:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used directly because the embeddings were already normalized to unit length, so the dot product was equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, the embeddings are not normalized by default, so you need to normalize them first; otherwise, the dot product is not cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: If embeddings are already unit-length, is dot product the same as cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
Yes. If both embeddings are already unit-length, then **dot product = cosine similarity**.

That’s because cosine similarity is:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

If \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\), this becomes just:

\[
a \cdot b
\]

So with normalized embeddings, you can use the dot product directly.

question: For vector search, is it better to embed just the answer text, just the question, or both together?
answer_llm:
There’s no single right answer — it’s something to test on your dataset.

The course suggests trying three options:

- embed the answer text only
- embed the question only
- embed both together as `question + " " + text`

Often, embedding both works best, but it also makes the input longer and slightly more expensive. The right choice is whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set.

question: How does cosine similarity measure how close two vectors are in text search?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures how close two vectors are by looking at the **angle between them**, not their raw size.

- If two vectors point in the **same direction**, cosine similarity is **1**.
- If they are **unrelated/orthogonal**, it is **0**.
- If they point in **opposite directions**, it is **-1**.

In text search, documents and queries are turned into vectors. Cosine similarity tells you how similar their content is based on how aligned those vectors are. If the vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity is just the **dot product** between them.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why do people use cosine similarity for comparing documents instead of plain distance?
answer_llm:
People use cosine similarity because it compares the **direction** of document vectors, not their raw size. That’s useful in text search since two documents can be similar in content even if one is much longer than the other.

A plain distance measure is affected by vector magnitude, so longer documents can look “farther away” just because they have more words. Cosine similarity avoids that by focusing on how aligned the vectors are.

When vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity is equivalent to a dot product, which also makes it efficient to compute.

question: What does it mean when cosine similarity is 1, 0, or -1?
answer_llm:
When cosine similarity is:

- **1**: the vectors point in the **same direction** — they are identical in orientation.
- **0**: the vectors are **orthogonal** — they have no similarity.
- **-1**: the vectors point in **exactly opposite directions** — completely opposite.

- Large angle → vectors point in different directions → texts are less similar

question: Why does cosine similarity turn into just a dot product once the embeddings are normalized?
answer_llm:
Once the embeddings are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes are all 1. Cosine similarity is:

\[
\frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

If \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\), then this becomes just:

\[
a \cdot b
\]

So after normalization, cosine similarity is exactly the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: In vector search, why can we use matrix multiplication to score all documents against one query?
answer_llm: Because each document embedding can be compared to the query with a dot product, and if the vectors are normalized, that dot product is the same as cosine similarity. Then all document scores can be computed at once as a matrix multiplication: the document matrix times the query vector.

This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: What changes when document and query vectors are normalized before computing cosine similarity?
answer_llm: When document and query vectors are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer affect the result. Cosine similarity then becomes equivalent to the dot product between the vectors, so it can be computed efficiently with matrix multiplication.

question: How does unit-length normalization make cosine similarity easier to calculate efficiently?
answer_llm:
By normalizing all vectors to unit length, their magnitudes no longer affect the result. That means cosine similarity becomes just the dot product between the query vector and each document vector.

This is efficient because dot products can be computed in one matrix multiplication instead of calculating angles separately for every pair of vectors.

question: Why is cosine similarity between a query and embeddings the same as a matrix multiply in this case?
answer_llm:
Because the embeddings and the query are **normalized to unit length**.

When vectors are normalized, cosine similarity becomes just the **dot product**:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|} \rightarrow a \cdot b
\]

since \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\).

So instead of computing cosine similarity one pair at a time, you can stack all document embeddings into a matrix and do a **matrix multiply** with the query vector to get all similarities at once.

After that, `download.py` will see the file already exists locally and skip re-downloading it.

question: Why does the vector search result from the matrix multiply and the loop not match exactly, even though the code is the same math?
answer_llm:
The results don’t match exactly because of **floating-point precision**.

Even if the math is the same, `X.dot(v_query)` and the explicit loop can sum the numbers in a **different order**. Floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, so you may get tiny differences in the last decimal places. The values are still effectively the same.

Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

rather than expecting exact equality.
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Is it normal for `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` to return `False` when I compare dot products from `X.dot(v_query)` and a for-loop?

`X.dot(v_query)` and the for-loop compute the same dot products, but they may add numbers in a different order. Because floating-point arithmetic isn’t perfectly associative, the results can differ slightly in the last few decimal places.

So `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` may return `False` if the default tolerance is too strict. Try:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

The values are usually not wrong; it’s just floating-point precision.

question: For Module 2 HW Q2, my cosine similarity doesn’t match any choice — is there a different model I’m supposed to use?
answer_llm:
Yes — for Module 2 HW Q2 you’re supposed to use the homework’s `Embedder` from `embedder.py`, not a different sentence-transformers model.

Use:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Common reasons the cosine similarity doesn’t match an option:

- you used a different embedding model than the homework specifies
- you embedded the wrong text field instead of the page’s `content`
- you didn’t compare against the Q1 query vector
- you expected a different similarity formula

The homework embedder returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly. If your value is still a little off, pick the closest option.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up the Gemini key for Kestra, and do I need both a normal env var and a SECRET_ one?
answer_llm:
Yes — for Kestra you typically need **both**:

- a **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the **AI Copilot**
- a **base64-encoded** `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the **flows**

Example:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In a flow, reference it **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

So yes: **plain env var + SECRET_ one**.
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why isn’t Kestra seeing my API key after I added it, and do I need to restart Docker Compose?
answer_llm:
Kestra won’t see the new API key unless:

- you exported it in the same shell session used to start Docker Compose,
- the value is encoded correctly, and
- you restarted Kestra/Docker Compose after adding it.

A common mistake is a bad base64 value from a trailing newline/space. Use:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

So yes, **you do need to restart Docker Compose** for Kestra to pick up the new environment variables.

Also, in flows reference it as:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating Kestra flows, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
It usually means Kestra AI Copilot didn’t get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to that canned refusal.

What to check first:
- Make sure you exported the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` before starting Kestra.
- Then restart Kestra.

Example:
```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Also check:
- `echo $GEMINI_API_KEY` in the same shell where you ran `docker compose up`
- that the key is correct and not rate-limited

Important: the Copilot uses the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`, not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).

question: Why does Kestra say my Gemini key is missing even though I set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
Kestra says the key is missing because it likely isn’t reading a valid secret value, even though `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is set.

The most common issue is a bad base64 encoding, usually caused by a trailing newline or space when you create the secret. Use `echo -n`:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also check these:

- Restart Kestra after exporting the variable:
  ```bash
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- Make sure you reference it in flows without the `SECRET_` prefix:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```
- Export it in the same shell session where you start Kestra.

If needed, put the values in a `.env` file and start with:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Could a bad base64 encode be breaking my secret in Kestra? I used normal echo, is that a problem?
answer_llm:
Yes — a bad base64 encode can break the secret in Kestra.

The most common issue is using `echo` instead of `echo -n`, because `echo` adds a trailing newline, which gets encoded too and can corrupt the secret.

Use this instead:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also make sure:

- you restart Kestra after exporting the variable
- you reference it in the flow as `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`
- the variable is exported in the same shell session before starting Kestra

If you already used plain `echo`, re-encode it with `-n` and try again.

question: What’s the easiest way to pass Gemini and other keys to Kestra, maybe with a .env file?
answer_llm:
Yes — the easiest way is to keep your keys in a `.env` file and pass it to Kestra when starting Docker Compose.

For example:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

For Kestra specifically, remember that secrets need to be available as environment variables prefixed with `SECRET_`, and the value must be base64-encoded.

Example `.env`:

```bash

SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=...
SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=...
SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=...
```

And for Gemini you can generate the secret like this:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Then start Kestra again so it picks up the values.

Also:

- don’t commit `.env` to Git
- use `.env.example` with only variable names
- in flows, reference secrets without the `SECRET_` prefix, like:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

question: Why do we need an orchestrator like Kestra instead of just running everything in a notebook?
answer_llm:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but for real workflows you usually need more than code that runs once. An orchestrator like Kestra gives you:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- reliable chaining of multiple tasks

So the main reason is production reliability and automation.

In this course, Kestra is also used to demonstrate AI workflows in a production-style setup, like generating flows with an AI copilot, grounding with RAG, and using agents that call tools.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: Why do I get `JSONDecodeError: Expecting value` when I try to parse the LLM output with `json.loads` while creating ground-truth questions?
answer_llm:
Because the LLM output is not always pure JSON. It may wrap the JSON in a markdown code fence or include extra prose before/after it, so `json.loads(response)` fails with:

```python
JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

To avoid that, force the model to return JSON only:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also make the prompt explicit, for example:

```text
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

If you want, I can also show how to make this work with Gemini or Groq.
answer_orig:
The LLM sometimes wraps the JSON in a markdown code fence or adds prose around it, so `json.loads(response)` fails with:

```

```

Force JSON output with OpenAI's `response_format`:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also be explicit in the prompt about the expected shape:

```

Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

Most providers have an equivalent (Gemini's `response_mime_type="application/json"`, Groq's `response_format`, etc.).

The LLM sometimes wraps the JSON in a markdown code fence or adds prose around it, so `json.loads(response)` fails with:

```
JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

Force JSON output with OpenAI's `response_format`:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also be explicit in the prompt about the expected shape:

```
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

Most providers have an equivalent (Gemini's `response_mime_type="application/json"`, Groq's `response_format`, etc.).

question: Why does my Jupyter kernel keep crashing when I try to embed the ground-truth set?
answer_llm:
Your kernel is probably crashing because you’re running out of memory. On small-RAM machines, loading an embedding model and embedding the full ground-truth set at the same time can exceed available RAM.

Workarounds:
- Use a smaller embedder like `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2`
- Move embedding into a separate Python script and load the saved vectors back into the notebook
- Use a machine with more RAM, or run locally
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches with `del` and `gc.collect()`

If you switch models, re-run the evaluation because the hit-rate / MRR will change.

- Move the embedding step into a separate Python script that you run from the terminal, then load the saved vectors back into the notebook.
- Use a Codespaces machine type with more RAM (Settings → "Machine type" on a Codespace), or run locally.
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches (`del`, `gc.collect()`).

question: Why do I still get rate-limit errors on Gemini even after adding a sleep between requests?
answer_llm:
Because Gemini has **both per-minute and per-day free-tier limits**. A `sleep(4)` only slows you down enough to help with the **per-minute** quota, but a long loop can still consume the **daily** quota and trigger rate-limit errors anyway.

What you can do:
- use a cheaper/higher-limit model like `gpt-4o-mini`
- use Groq’s free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`)
- lower concurrency if using threads
- save progress and resume later
answer_orig:
Free-tier Gemini limits both per-minute and per-day requests. Adding `time.sleep(4)` only fixes the per-minute side — a long `tqdm` loop can still blow through the per-day quota in one run.

Options when this happens:

- Spend ~$5 on OpenAI and use `gpt-4o-mini`. It's cheap enough to embed/generate the entire ground-truth set and has higher rate limits.
- Use Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) — generous request-per-minute limits.
- Lower concurrency for thread-pool calls. Use a smaller pool size (2–3 workers) instead of pushing the API hard.
- Resume from where you stopped. Save progress periodically (e.g. dump the partial results to a JSONL file) so a hit limit doesn't lose all work.

question: What's the best way to avoid losing all progress if my ground-truth generation gets interrupted by rate limits?
answer_llm:
The best way is to **save progress periodically and resume from where you left off**.

In practice, write partial ground-truth results out as you generate them, for example to a **JSONL file**, so if you hit a rate limit you don’t lose the whole run.

The context also suggests a few ways to reduce how often this happens:
- use a provider with higher limits, like **OpenAI `gpt-4o-mini`** or **Groq’s free tier**
- **lower concurrency** if you’re using a thread pool
- note that `time.sleep(4)` only helps with per-minute limits, not the per-day quota

So the key answer is: **checkpoint your outputs and resume later**.

question: How do I make Pandas stop truncating long text in a column and show the full content?
answer_llm:
Use:

```python
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
```

This tells Pandas not to truncate long text in columns and to show the full content.
answer_orig:
By default, Pandas truncates text content in a column to 50 characters. To view the entire explanation provided by the judge LLM for a non-relevant answer, use the following instruction:

```python
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
```

- **Option:** `display.max_colwidth`
- **Type:** `int` or `None`
- **Description:** Sets the maximum width in characters of a column in the representation of a pandas data structure. When a column overflows, a "..." placeholder is used in the output. Setting it to 'None' allows unlimited width.
- **Default:** 50

Refer to the [official documentation](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/options.html) for more details.

question: How can I normalize vectors stored in a Pandas DataFrame column?

You can normalize vectors in a Pandas DataFrame column with `apply` and NumPy’s norm:

```python
import numpy as np

normalize_vec = lambda v: v / np.linalg.norm(v)

df["new_col"] = df["org_col"].apply(normalize_vec)
```

This creates a new column with the normalized vectors.

question: Why do I get "streamlit: executable file not found in $PATH" when starting my app with docker-compose, even though it works locally?
answer_llm:
This usually happens because Docker Compose is starting the container without the app’s runtime environment being built into the image. Locally, `streamlit` is available in your shell, but inside the container it isn’t on `$PATH`.

The fix is:

1. Make sure you have a `Dockerfile`.
2. Add `streamlit` to your `docker-compose` setup.
3. Rebuild the image and start it again:

```bash
docker-compose up --build
```

So the key point is: it works locally because Streamlit is installed on your machine, but in Docker it must be installed/configured in the container.
answer_orig:
To resolve this issue:

1. Ensure you have created a `Dockerfile`.
2. Add `streamlit` to the `docker-compose` configuration.
3. Run the following command to rebuild and start the application:

   ```bash
   docker-compose up --build
   ```

question: Can the capstone be done in a team, or does each person need their own project?
answer_llm:
The capstone is **individual**, not a team project.

You can **collaborate or discuss ideas** with other students, but each submitted project must be **independent**, with a **separate qualifying component** and **separate repository** if there’s any shared system. If it can’t be evaluated separately for each person, it doesn’t meet the requirement.
answer_orig:
No, the capstone is an individual project.

You can collaborate or discuss a larger idea with other students, but each submitted project must stand on its own. A shared system can work only if it is clearly decomposed into independent projects, where each person has a separate qualifying component and a separate repository.

If the work cannot be evaluated independently for each participant, it does not satisfy the project requirement.

question: Do I actually need Airflow or Mage for the capstone ingestion part, or is a simple Python script enough?

question: How can I evaluate a recommender-style RAG project when I don’t have any clear ground-truth Q&A pairs?
answer_llm:
Use two complementary evaluation methods:

1. Synthetic ground truth  
   For each item in your catalog, prompt an LLM with the item description and ask it to generate about 5 user queries that should retrieve that item as the top result. Then run those queries through your retriever and measure metrics like hit rate, MRR, or NDCG.

2. LLM-as-a-judge  
   Sample queries, run the full RAG/recommender pipeline, and have an LLM score the result for relevance/usefulness using a fixed rubric, like a 1–5 scale.

For recommender-style problems, NDCG is often better than hit rate because it rewards good items appearing near the top of the ranked list, not just at rank 1.
answer_orig:
Two complementary approaches that both score for the evaluation criterion:

1. Synthetic ground truth (same idea as the course, adapted). For each item in your dataset, prompt the LLM with the item's description and ask it to generate ~5 user queries that should return that item as the top result. Then run those queries through your retrieval and measure hit rate / MRR / NDCG.

2. LLM-as-a-judge for end-to-end quality. Sample queries, run the full RAG, and have an LLM rate the result for relevance/usefulness on a fixed rubric (e.g. 1–5 scale, with criteria you specify in the prompt).

NDCG is often a better fit than hit-rate for ranking-style problems where multiple items are acceptable answers — it rewards getting good items high in the list, not just first.

question: If I’m checking retrieval quality in a recommender setup, should I use hit rate, MRR, or NDCG?
answer_llm:
It depends on your setup, but for a recommender-style retrieval problem:

- **Hit rate** is good if you only care whether the right item appears anywhere in the top-k.
- **MRR** is better if the position of the first relevant item matters.
- **NDCG** is often the **best fit** for ranking-style recommender retrieval, especially when **multiple items can be acceptable** and you want to reward getting the best ones higher in the list.

So in general: **use NDCG if you’re evaluating ranking quality**, and compare with **hit rate / MRR** as complementary metrics if useful.

question: How do people use an LLM as a judge to score the output of a RAG recommender end to end?
answer_llm:
People use an **LLM-as-a-judge** by:

1. **Sampling queries** from real or synthetic users.
2. **Running the full RAG recommender end to end** on each query.
3. **Asking another LLM to score the output** against a fixed rubric, such as:
   - relevance
   - usefulness
   - whether the recommendation matches the user intent
   - a **1–5 scale** or similar

You then aggregate those scores across queries to get an overall quality measure.

A common pattern is to include clear criteria in the prompt, for example: “Rate the result from 1 to 5 for relevance and usefulness. Explain briefly why.”

```python

```

question: For ranking-type RAG problems where more than one answer is okay, why is NDCG often a better metric than hit rate?
answer_llm:
NDCG is often better because it accounts for **ranking quality**, not just whether a relevant item appears somewhere in the results.

For ranking-style RAG problems where **multiple answers are acceptable**:

- **Hit rate** only checks if at least one good item is present.
- **NDCG** rewards putting the **best items higher** in the list and can handle **graded relevance**.

So if several results are acceptable, NDCG gives a more informative score than hit rate, since it reflects how well the system ranks the good answers.

question: How should I chunk a really big PDF corpus without overthinking it first?
answer_llm:
Don’t try to find the perfect chunker upfront — iterate.

A good starting strategy is:

- **Start simple:** fixed-size chunks of about **1000 tokens** with some **overlap**
- **Run a small ground-truth eval**
- Then try:
  - **Smart chunking**: split documents into logical sections with an LLM
  - **Chunk summaries**: add a short LLM-generated summary per chunk and index it too
  - **Hybrid search** for long structured docs like legal/financial PDFs: **BM25 + dense retrieval**

If your PDFs are messy, first convert them to clean markdown using tools like:

- `pymupdf4llm`
- Docling
- GROBID for academic papers

Then **re-run the eval after each change** and optimize for **hit rate / MRR**, not for a “perfect” chunker in the abstract.
answer_orig:
Don't try to find the perfect chunker upfront — iterate.

- Start simple: fixed-size chunking (~1000 tokens with some overlap) and run a small ground-truth eval.
- Try smart chunking: ask an LLM to split each document into logical sections, then index each section.
- Add a short LLM-generated summary per chunk and index it alongside, or use it to boost retrieval.
- For long, structured documents (legal, financial), prefer hybrid search (BM25 + dense) so exact wording isn't lost during semantic matching.

Useful tools for parsing PDFs to clean markdown before chunking:

- [`pymupdf4llm`](https://pypi.org/project/pymupdf4llm/) — fast, decent quality.
- [Docling](https://github.com/docling-project/docling) — slower but higher quality on tables/figures.
- [GROBID](https://github.com/kermitt2/grobid) — for academic papers, extracts structure (sections, refs, etc.).

Run the eval again after each change. The goal is measurable improvement on hit rate / MRR for your ground-truth set, not a "perfect" chunker in the abstract.

question: What’s a simple chunking setup to start with for long docs, and how do I know if it’s working?
answer_llm:
Start simple with **fixed-size chunks of about 1000 tokens, with some overlap**.

To see if it’s working, don’t judge it abstractly — **run a small ground-truth eval** on a few known Q&A pairs and measure **hit rate / MRR**. Then:

1. Build the simple chunker.
2. Evaluate it on your ground-truth set.
3. Change one thing at a time.
4. Re-run the eval after each change.

A good sign it’s working is that your **retrieval metrics improve** after each iteration.

If you want to improve beyond the baseline, the next steps are:
- split docs into logical sections with an LLM,
- add short summaries per chunk,
- use **hybrid search (BM25 + dense)** for long structured docs.
